title_en: Hadiths on Social Conduct, Commerce, and Ethics
content_en:
At-Tirmidhi's Jami' is as much a book of ethics and social conduct as it is a collection of legal hadiths. Al-Mubarakpuri's commentary on the chapters dealing with commerce (buyuu'), marriage (nikah), manners (adab), and governance brings out the full richness of these traditions and their implications for Muslim social life.

The chapters on commerce begin with the famous hadith warning against usurious transactions and the statement of the Prophet, peace be upon him, that both the one who pays and the one who receives riba (interest) are cursed, along with the witness and the scribe. Al-Mubarakpuri explains the categories of riba in Islamic law, distinguishing riba al-fadl (the exchange of like commodities in unequal amounts) from riba an-nasiah (deferred payment with increase), and traces the jurisprudential disagreements about which commodities fall under the prohibition.

On marriage, the commentary addresses the hadith emphasizing the importance of choosing a spouse for her religion above her wealth, lineage, and beauty. Al-Mubarakpuri uses this as a starting point for a discussion of the objectives of Islamic marriage — producing righteous children, maintaining chastity, and building the foundations of a stable community — while noting that the hadith does not absolutely prohibit considering other qualities in a spouse.

The chapters on manners in the Jami' are among the most beloved sections of the collection, and al-Mubarakpuri's commentary on them reflects his care for the spiritual and ethical dimensions of the prophetic example. The hadith that the best among you in character is the most beloved to the Prophet receives extended discussion, with al-Mubarakpuri surveying what the scholars have considered the components of good character: gentleness, patience, generosity, truthfulness, and concern for others.

On governance, the hadiths about obeying those in authority and the conditions under which disobedience becomes obligatory (commanding what is clearly forbidden) receive careful treatment. Al-Mubarakpuri navigates this sensitive territory by presenting the mainstream Sunni position — that rebellion against an unjust ruler is generally prohibited due to the greater harm it causes — while acknowledging the scholars who placed limits on this obligation.
